summary:
The Battle of Trafalgar, fought on 21 October 1805 off the southwestern coast of Spain near Cape Trafalgar, was the decisive naval engagement of the [[event:109|Napoleonic Wars]]. Vice-Admiral Horatio Nelson led the British fleet of 27 ships of the line against a combined Franco-Spanish fleet of 33 vessels under French Admiral Pierre-Charles Villeneuve. The battle secured British naval supremacy for over a century and permanently dashed Napoleon's ambitions of invading England.

Nelson's tactical approach was unconventional and audacious. Rather than following the standard practice of forming a parallel line of battle, he divided his fleet into two columns and sailed them perpendicular to the Franco-Spanish line, breaking it at two points and engaging the enemy in a close-quarters melee where British gunnery and crew experience would be decisive. The tactic was high risk — his leading ships were exposed to concentrated fire during their approach — but it proved devastatingly effective once the lines were broken.

The British achieved a lopsided victory, capturing or destroying 22 of the 33 enemy ships without losing a single vessel of their own. Approximately 4,500 Franco-Spanish sailors were killed and around 7,000 captured. Yet the battle came at a profound personal cost: Nelson was struck by a musket ball fired from the French ship Redoutable and died aboard HMS Victory as the battle was being won. His final signal to the fleet — "England expects that every man will do his duty" — became one of the most celebrated phrases in British history.

Trafalgar's strategic consequences were enormous. Britain remained unchallenged at sea for the rest of the Napoleonic Wars and beyond, able to impose a naval blockade on France and supply its allies and expeditionary forces at will. Napoleon responded by attempting to defeat Britain economically through the Continental System, which barred British goods from European ports, but this policy proved difficult to enforce and generated widespread resentment among his allies.
